tokens: (Faint, under a great deal of static) Houston, Apollo 16.
Over.
(Long Pause) Houston, Apollo 16.
Over.
(Pause) Houston, Orion.
Over.
(Long Pause) Houston; Orion.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Orion, Houston.
How do you read?
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Rog.
What time are we supposed to get up?
Over.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: John, I can't understand you.
We'd like, if you're reading, to go Down Voice Backup.
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay.
What time is wake-up time?
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: You're about 3-1/2 minutes from nominal wake-up now.
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Okay, we timed it pretty good.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Looks like you timed it just about right on your own.
(Long Pause; static clears) Orion, Houston.
Voice check.
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: (Muddy, but improved) We're reading you loud and clear on Down Voice Backup.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
We're reading you a little better, too.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: How did the systems look last night?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Everything looked real good, John.
transcript.speaker: Peterson

tokens: Hello, Houston; 15 (sic).
(Long Pause) Hello, Houston; 15.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Good morning, Charlie.
How are you?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I guess it's 16; excuse me.
(Hearing Tony) Fine.
I guess we're 16.
How did the biomed look on me all night long, Tony?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, Charlie.
I guess the biomed looked good.
How do you feel this morning?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I feel great.
Why don't you have the docs tell me how much they think I slept?
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, they say they think you've slept 6 hours.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, fine.
I was going to say 7.
Of course, I don't know exactly when we got started, but once we got started, it was just like a baby, except for...One time, I woke when I got cold, and had to put on my sleeping bag.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Outstanding.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay; here we go with the crew status report.
First, I'd like to say that we gorged ourselves, really, and still couldn't eat everything.
I tell you, one of those meals would fill the whole Roman army on maneuvers for 2 days.
But John, he ate, yesterday: Day 5 Meal C, and Day 6 Meal A.
And on Day 5 Meal C, John ate everything, plus an EVA beverage.
And the Day 6 meal A, which was breakfast yesterday, John ate everything except the ham steak.
You can scratch the ham steak.
He got 7 hours and 15 minutes sleep last night, and he took no medication.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay; we got that, Charlie.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay; turning to my page.
On Day 5 Meal C, you can scratch the chocolate pudding, and I ate everything else plus the EVA beverage.
And John, also, had an EVA beverage.
On Day 6 Meal A, I ate everything but the ham steak.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay; we copy.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: And I said I got 7 hours, but whatever you all say.
I slept real good; feel great.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay; before you all get too covered up (with the suit), we'd like you to check your biomed sensors.
(Pause) And did you take any medication?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Yeah, I took another Seconal again, to start off.
And I guess my biomed (sensor) is good; John's look firm as they can be.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Now we're going to start the chow.
And, well, I guess I'll copy the lift-off times first, if you have them.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Say again, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Do you have those lift-off times for us?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay; stand by one.
(Long Pause) Okay, Apollo 16.
We don't have that block time yet.
We'll get it to you in a minute.
We have a couple of questions about the (ALSEP) heat flow (experiment) again, if you want to think about those.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
That heat flow cable that fits into the Central Station is connected to a printed circuit board inside the connector there.
And we're curious about the end of the cable.
Does it have any of that printed circuit board on it or is it just a free end of the ribbon cable?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Tony, I was thinking about that last night, and it looked like to me that it might have some of that printed circuit.
All I can see is that the end of the cable is very smooth and it's silver and I think the printed circuit is partly silver, but I'm not really positive; that's something you can think about.
And it is very smooth; and around the connector it is very smooth there, also.
And as John kicked that thing out of there, it came right out of there without moving the Central Station at all!
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
How about the PSE - the passive seismic cable?
Is it tight?
What we have to decide here is, if you move the Central Station, will it disturb the PSE?
That thing (meaning the sensors in the seismometer) is uncaged now and you can't re-cage it.
So we can't afford to move it.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Well, he said he thinks it's pretty tight, John does; but I can't remember.
How far do you want to move it?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, what we're thinking about is if we did ask you to take off that astroconnector on the heat flow experiment, you may have to lift the station up to get underneath to the little lever.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Yeah.
But, Tony, everybody's told me it's serrated and once you got that little beauty on there, you can't get it off!
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're working on that now.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Yeah, I think there's a little pin or something in there you have to push in.
And I don't know whether we got the tools for that.
(Pause) Well, I think we could do that.
It's not that tight.
I think we could do that; pick it up.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay; understand.
Fredo's (Fred Haise) been over running, suited, this heat flow experiment, to see if the procedure is effective.
We don't have a decision yet.
We don't want to get you all excited about it, but we'll have something - a yes or no - in about 45 minutes.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Well, I guess we'll still be here.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Tony, if I thought yesterday's meal was big, you ought to see this one.
Man, there's a lot of chow here.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, which meal did you say you're eating?
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: It's breakfast.
Day 7 meal A...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
(Pause) We're just trying to put a little weight on you.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (Lost under Tony) Day 6, Meal B tonight.
Man, I tell you, I feel "oink" right now.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I can just sit here and talk a few words about the traverse today, if you like.
I haven't really organized my notes on it, so it may ramble a little bit, but I've gotten a briefing by the planning team.
It looks like an interesting plan here.
The traverse is almost...Well, it's exactly like normal EVA-2.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (The Approving Teacher) There you go.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (Chuckles) From Ken (Mattingly)'s words, he can see definite benches in Stone Mountain and thinks that you'll have a pretty good chance of identifying them.
He can see layers in South Ray, which makes the rays at Station 8 a whole lot more interesting than we had even anticipated.
So right now, we'd like you to do a normal (Stations) 4, 5, and 6.
The main thing identifying 6 is being on the bottom of the slope of the lowest bench, Station 5 being on top of the first bench, and Station 4 being on top of the second bench.
In fact, it may be even a little above.
Right now, we're thinking that we won't have you aim for Crown, because Crown is probably a little bit more subdued than we'd like, and probably not worth the effort to get to.
Cinco D and E is, as we talked about before...(will) probably be Station 4 (as planned).
It'll probably be a good place.
At the end of Station 5, we may have you do an LPM (Lunar Portable Magnetometer).
I'll update this all in real time for you, I'm just talking about what we'll probably do here.
And Station 7 we're going to eliminate and take the whole time and put it at Station 10 so you'll be able to do that LM-area sampling you asked for; we think that sounds great.

As you drive (west) from 6 to 8, we're more interested now that you do get some pictures of Stubby as you go along - of that area - because we don't have Station 7; so we'll ask you to turn your DAC (Data Acquisition Camera, the 16-mm movie camera) over that way as you're driving.
A DAC pointed down-Sun probably wouldn't see much, anyway, in that zero phase.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, Tony, I think you'll be able to...From up on Stone there, it looks like to me you're gonna be able to see right into South Ray and right into Stubby and all those craters.
The general topography here is a downslope.
From North Ray, it leads from the ridge to our immediate right all the way down to South Ray, with the lowest point really being maybe south of Survey Ridge.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, right.
And we could see, or I think I could see, the bright area of South Ray on the TV pan.
It really stands out down there.

But our really exciting station looks like it may be Station 8, and we're really going to encourage you to scout around and see if you can get the samples of the different layers that we see in South Ray.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: It looks to us like the base of Stone Mountain is really topographically lower than we are right now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
(Pause) And, also, on Station 9, it may turn out to be difficult to find a pristine area.
We'll let you scout around a little bit, and pick your own Station 9 on whatever looks like isn't ray material.
And on the location of Station 10, we'd like you to put it just about where you said it might go as you were driving back yesterday.

It sounds like the contact between the ray and the non-ray material is just west of you there, and we'd like to run the penetrometer array along the contact but in the ray material; that is, the penetrometer array would now go in a northeast/southwest direction.
And a double core would be something like 50 meters to the southwest of the deep core.
(Pause) I may correct that (in) a second here.
Hold on.
(Pause) I'll correct that.
The double core will be about 50 meters towards the LM but along the contact, so it'll be about 50 meters closer to the LM than the deep drill.

(Pause) And at Station 10, since you'll have a longer sampling time, we'd like you to sample on both sides of the ray; in other words, in the ray, along the line between the deep drill core and the LM, and off the ray to the west.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (Changing subjects) Okay, Houston.
We're going to have to move the UV camera back.
I think the front lens is starting to get into the Sun.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Say again, John.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, we've got a plan here at the end of the EVA to move it back.
Do you think it will stay out of the Sun until then?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Did you get that, Houston?
(Pause) Houston, 16.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Go ahead.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Houston, did you copy John?
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We sure did there, Charlie.
You copy us?
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Orion, this is Honeysuckle (Australia).
We have a comm outage with Houston at this time.
Stand by one, please.
transcript.speaker: Saxon

tokens: (Under heavy static) Okay, Honeysuckle.
Nice to talk to you.
How are y'all doing down there?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We're doing great; nice to talk to you.
transcript.speaker: Saxon

tokens: Right; you sound good.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger that; we'll be with you shortly.
We're just getting some line (garbled).
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Saxon

tokens: Have a Swan for us.
transcript.speaker: Young

I'm sorry about this.

transcript.speaker: Saxon

tokens: Okay, you're loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger that.
I read you a bit better now.

transcript.speaker: Saxon

tokens: John was saying, 'Have a Swan for us'.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Saxon

transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Houston, Comm Tech Honeysuckle Net 1.
And how do you read?
transcript.speaker: Saxon

tokens: Honeysuckle, Houston.
Comm test.
Loud and clear.
How do you read?
transcript.speaker: Houston

tokens: Houston, Comm Tech Honeysuckle net 1.
How do you read?
(Pause) Orion, this is Honeysuckle.
We're still having a little trouble with the incoming from Houston.
We should get it restored very shortly for you.
Sorry about the delay.
transcript.speaker: Saxon

tokens: Roger.
Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay, we're reading you this time for a change.
transcript.speaker: Saxon

tokens: Okay.
You guys are nice to talk to; we don't care about Houston.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, thanks very much.
Certainly appreciate it.
It has been a pleasure working on this mission.
transcript.speaker: Saxon

tokens: Roger.
We'd sure like to come down there and see you folks after it's over, too.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Well, you've got a permanent invite, any time you like.
transcript.speaker: Saxon

tokens: That's very kind.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We'll keep the beer cool for you.
transcript.speaker: Saxon

tokens: Honeysuckle Comm Tech, Houston Comm Tech, Net 1.
transcript.speaker: Houston

tokens: You just got a couple of fellows going to show up on your lawn here (garbled).
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: That (meaning a beer)'s the best idea I've heard all day.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Thanks.
It's a pretty good one down here, too.
transcript.speaker: Saxon

tokens: You see, in my terminology, (I would like some) friendly 48-packs, right now, the way I feel.
I'd really love one.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger that.
We're still having difficulty with the comm, Orion.
Hopefully, very shortly, we'll get the net up for you.
transcript.speaker: Saxon

tokens: Okay.
Take your time.
Don't worry about it; we're eating.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Oh, don't worry, Orion; we're worrying.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Saxon

tokens: Goddard Voice, Houston Comm Tech on (garbled)
transcript.speaker: Houston

tokens: Roger.
Goddard Voice.
transcript.speaker: Saxon

tokens: Roger.
How do you read?
transcript.speaker: Houston

tokens: Read you loud and clear.
How me?
transcript.speaker: Saxon

tokens: I read you same.
transcript.speaker: Houston

tokens: Roger.
We'll check on the circuit.
transcript.speaker: Saxon

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Houston

tokens: Honeysuckle Comm Tech, Houston Comm Tech.
Contact Net 1 for a comm check.
transcript.speaker: Houston

tokens: Loud and clear.
Comm check on me.
transcript.speaker: Saxon

tokens: You're loud and clear, also.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Houston

tokens: Orion, Houston.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Hey, outstanding!
You're back.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Yeah, you were gone for a little while, and we had a nice chat with Honeysuckle.
They're mighty friendly folks down there.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Very good.
Hey, during some of that, I was chatting away about EVA-2.
I wonder how much of it you got?
Did you get on through to Station 10?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: No, we stopped at about Station 8, Tony.
Well, actually, you were talking about taking pictures of Stubby as we go by Station 7; and that was all.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, fine.
If you'd like, I'll continue on with that.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, Tony.
You know, in real time, how we're going to have to do this: you guys are going to have to tell us, when we get to a place, what you want done when we get there.
There's no way we can remember it or write it down.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Oh, I understand that.
And we'll do all of it real time, but I just thought that you might like an overall plan before you get out.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay; that's a good idea.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
As you drive from Station 6 to 8, we'd like for you to swing that DAC (Data Acquisition Camera) over and take pictures into Stubby, and all points to the south.
And then we're going to really stress Station 8, because it may be a chance to sample many of those materials that Ken was able to see in South Ray, so Station 8 is a key station.
Station 9, we're not at all sure that you'll be able to rec(gonize)...The nominal Station 9 is a pristine area, so we'll just let you scout around and see if you can find the best you can.
And Station 10 will be longer.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I was going to say, Tony.
I guarantee you we'll find a place that's out of sight of the LM that's 3 or 4 kilometers away.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, good show.
And Station 10 will be 12 to 15 minutes longer (than planned) now, and we'd still like it on a line between the deep core and the LM, so it will be on a northeast line.
And we'd like it in the ray that you described, but near enough to the edge so that you can sample off the west edge of the ray.
Also, we'd like you to pick up that vesicular basalt that you described underneath the engine bell.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay.
Hey, listen.
This is a pretty good size...I don't know how big this ray is, but for us to sample on the west edge of it (laughs), I don't understand how we're going to do that.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Where do you think that west edge was?
Was it all the way back towards Spook?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: It's not that far; but I think, like, if we go due west of the LM, we can go 300 meters and start running into some really big boulders, which are probably in the center of the ray, and then they thin out a little off to the other side...So, the ray...I think we're in about the three quarters of it, and it probably runs over that way for a good long ways.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: John, the comm was bad enough, I didn't get all that.
Maybe they got it in the Backroom back there, and we'll act on it (in) real time.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay.
All I'm saying is the ray must be 350 meters wide, and we're on probably the (pause) east half of it.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: When you get a chance up there, could you look out the window and see if the LRV battery covers are still open?
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Yeah, they're both still open, Tony.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, they're both still open.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Yeah; in fact, all three of them are open.
(Pause) Oh, that's right.
John just reminded me (that) two of them are hooked together, but they're all open anyway.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I think one problem, (garbled) analysis may not have figured, is that we probably are parked at about a four or five degree right roll.
So it put it more into the Sun than it would have been otherwise.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Right.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I have this block data here, if you want to take it sometime.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay.
(Garbled) my opinion, would probably be to sample the edge of ray, we gotta go east maybe about 100 meters.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I'm sorry, John.
The comm is real bad.
Say it again, please.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I said, it would probably be best to go east a hundred meters to sample the edge of the ray.
We'd be closer to the edge.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay, Tony.
Give me 2 seconds, and you start going with the update.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, the block data update.
(Pause) T-35, 142 plus 10 plus 51; T-36, 144 plus 09 plus 22; T-37, 146 plus 07 plus 53; T-38, 148 plus 06 plus 25; T-39, 150 plus 04 plus 57.
And that's it.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, Tony.
For some reason Rev 34 is blank.
We missed that last night, and we didn't get it this morning.
But starting with 35: 142 plus 10 plus 51; 144 plus 09 plus 22; 146 plus 07 plus 53; 1 plus 48 plus 06 plus 25; 150 plus 04 plus 57.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay; the only one I have question on was T-38:148 plus 06 plus 25.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: That's a Charlie; that's what I got.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay; good show.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Hey, is it Friday or Saturday down there?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You know, I had to think about that last night.
In fact, I had to ask Kathy.
It's Saturday.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay; thank you.
Saturday morning?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Rog.
It was a nice, humid, but sunny morning when I came in.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Good show.
What is your GET (Ground Elapsed Time) now?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Say again, Charlie?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (Speaking very slowly) What is the present GET?
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, it's about 10 minutes 'til eight in the morning.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (Lecturing) GET; Ground Elapsed Time.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Oh, EGT (sic).
GET is 140 plus 10.
(Pause) You'll have to excuse me down here.
I'm having to interpolate.
The comm is really bad.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (Stretching the truth) Well, Honeysuckle was reading us pretty good!
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Orion, Houston.
Are you going to carry your pliers out with you?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Yeah; John's got 'em in...(He) had them in his pocket last time.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, good show.
We may need that to work on the cosmic ray.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Hey, Tony?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Go ahead, John.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Did you hear what we said about the UV camera?
Over.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I got about the UV camera; and we got a procedure all ready that we'll probably ask you to move it back to the north at the end of EVA-2.
You don't think it will be in the Sun before that, do you?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: It's in the Sun right now.
The lens is in the...The imagery is in the Sun.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay, we'll probably have you do it right away when you get out, then.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Are you sure this is going to be a normal EVA-2!?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Yes, it's a nominal EVA-2 with the exceptions I talked about.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, we're just pulling your leg, Tony.
(Tony laughs) We'll just play it real time, Tony.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We're just going to have you rebuild ALSEP and the cosmic ray experiment; otherwise, it is a normal EVA.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: That's what we're saying.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: If we eat all this meal, Tony, we are not going to be able to get our suits on.
transcript.speaker: Duke
